critic_raw: 1. ONE YEAR OF DATA FROM 10,000 PATIENTS IS LIKELY INSUFFICIENT FOR WEARABLE SEQUENCE LEARNING AT THE NEURAL NETWORK SCALE: The design uses 10,000 patient records over one year. The neural network model processes time-series from wearable devices (heart rate, activity level) as sequences. Wearable devices generate data at high sampling rates — typically 1 Hz or faster for heart rate. A year of data from 10,000 patients yields very large raw data volume, but the number of unique patients (10,000) is relatively small for fine-tuning or training a neural network that generalizes across the demographic diversity of readmission-risk populations. More importantly, a one-year collection window means all patients share the same macro-temporal context — no multi-year seasonal variation in readmission baseline, no policy shifts, no generalization across admission cohorts. The test population is drawn from the same year as the training population, producing an evaluation that conflates within-cohort performance with generalization. 2. STRATIFIED RANDOM SPLIT ON PATIENTS WITH WEARABLE DATA COLLECTED DURING AND POST-HOSPITALIZATION: For the neural network to use wearable time-series as features, the data must be collected during or after hospitalization and up to the readmission decision point (30 days post-discharge). If readmission occurs within this 30-day window, the wearable data from during that 30-day period may partially overlap with the outcome period. The design does not specify the temporal window from which wearable features are extracted or whether features are truncated at the discharge date. Features derived from wearable data collected during the 30-day outcome window would constitute label leakage — the model would see health deterioration signals that are part of the readmission event itself. 3. FEATURE AGGREGATION OVER 'SPECIFIED WINDOWS' IS UNSPECIFIED: The design states 'features from wearable devices are derived through aggregation of statistics over specified windows.' The window specification is load-bearing: a 24-hour aggregate over the discharge day captures different information than a 7-day post-discharge window. If the aggregation window extends into the outcome period (post-discharge but within the 30-day readmission label), the features contain information from after the discharge event. The design provides no specification of window size, placement relative to discharge, or whether different patients have different effective window sizes due to varying lengths of stay. 4. LOGISTIC REGRESSION BASELINE USES 'CLINICAL DEMOGRAPHICS' WITHOUT SPECIFYING CLINICAL PREDICTORS: The baseline is described as 'a logistic regression model using clinical demographics' receiving 'equal tuning and modeling effort.' Clinical demographics for readmission prediction are ambiguous: this could mean age, sex, and race only (a weak baseline), or it could include primary diagnosis, comorbidity index, prior admission history, discharge disposition, and vital signs at discharge (a strong baseline that captures most of the variance in 30-day readmission). The baseline's strength determines whether the wearable data adds incremental value beyond what is already captured by static clinical information. Without specifying the feature set, the baseline is uninterpretable. 5. WEARABLE DATA AVAILABILITY BIAS IS NOT ADDRESSED: Patients who wear health monitoring devices post-discharge are not a random sample of the readmission-risk population. Wearable device adoption is higher among younger, more health-literate, more financially secure patients — precisely the demographic less likely to experience preventable 30-day readmissions. If the 10,000 patients with wearable data are systematically different from the patients at highest readmission risk, the model trained on this population will not generalize to the high-risk patients it is intended to identify. The design does not address whether the patient population with complete wearable data is representative of the target deployment population.
all_issues_raised: One-year single-cohort data provides no temporal generalization test — all patients share the same macro-temporal context and seasonal baseline
Wearable feature extraction window is unspecified relative to the discharge date — features from within the 30-day outcome window constitute label leakage
Aggregation window specification is load-bearing but unspecified, creating ambiguity about whether post-discharge wearable signals contaminate training features
Logistic regression baseline clinical feature set is underspecified — the breadth of clinical predictors determines whether wearable data adds incremental value
Wearable device adoption bias selects a non-representative patient population that differs systematically from high-risk readmission patients

critic_raw: 1. UNSUPERVISED ANOMALY DETECTION IS TRAINED WITHOUT LABELS BUT EVALUATED WITH LABELS — FUNDAMENTAL EVALUATION DESIGN INCONSISTENCY: The design specifies an autoencoder as the anomaly detection model and calls it 'unsupervised.' Autoencoders for anomaly detection are trained on normal operating data without fault labels, and anomalies are detected as high-reconstruction-error instances at inference time. However, the design uses maintenance logs as 'reliable ground truth' labels for evaluation. The problem is that the threshold for converting reconstruction error into an anomaly binary decision is a free parameter. The design does not specify whether this threshold is set on the validation set (which would require knowing fault labels during threshold selection, partially defeating the unsupervised premise) or by an unsupervised heuristic (e.g., top-K percentile reconstruction error). If the threshold is tuned on labeled validation data, the model is no longer purely unsupervised — it is semi-supervised — and the design's characterization of the method is misleading. 2. MEAN TIME TO FAILURE AS PRIMARY METRIC IS NOT COMPUTABLE FROM A STATIC HOLDOUT DATASET: The primary metric is Mean Time to Failure (MTTF). MTTF is a metric that requires tracking when failures are predicted relative to when they actually occur — it is an operational metric defined over a deployment timeline, not a static holdout evaluation. Computing MTTF from a stratified random split of historical data requires knowing the prediction lead time for each detected fault, which requires temporal ordering of predictions. If the test set is randomly shuffled (as a random split implies), temporal ordering is destroyed and MTTF cannot be computed meaningfully. The design appears to conflate a deployment-context metric with a static evaluation metric, which are not the same thing. 3. STRATIFIED RANDOM SPLIT DESTROYS TEMPORAL DEPENDENCIES THAT DEFINE FAULT PRECURSORS: The design uses a stratified random split to maintain fault class proportions across splits. But fault precursors — the anomalous sensor readings leading up to a failure — are definitionally temporally ordered with respect to the fault event. If sensor readings from the pre-fault trajectory are in the training set and the fault event is in the test set (or vice versa), the model cannot learn the temporal degradation pattern and the threshold tuned in validation may be calibrated on a different temporal context than the test set. For unsupervised anomaly detection in particular, the reconstruction error threshold is highly sensitive to the distribution of 'normal' data in training; randomly mixing normal and pre-fault data corrupts the normal operating distribution. 4. THRESHOLD-BASED BASELINE TUNING IS UNSPECIFIED AGAINST A POTENTIALLY PRODUCTION-GRADE COMPETITOR: The design states both methods 'undergo equivalent tuning.' A threshold-based industrial sensor alert system can be as simple as a fixed setpoint or as sophisticated as a multi-variate statistical process control chart with learned control limits. The sophistication of the threshold baseline determines the validity of the comparison. Production industrial IoT monitoring systems use adaptive thresholds, multivariate anomaly scoring, and physics-based process models. If the threshold baseline is a simple static threshold, the autoencoder is being compared against a strawman, not against the systems currently deployed on industrial machinery. 5. PRECISION AT TOP-K ANOMALIES AS A SECONDARY METRIC IS INADEQUATELY SPECIFIED: The design mentions 'precision at top-K anomalies' as a secondary metric. This requires defining K: is it a fixed number of anomalies, a fixed fraction of the test set, or a business-process-defined quantity tied to maintenance team capacity? Different values of K will produce very different precision values, and if K is not specified before evaluation, the metric can be reported at the most favorable K. Furthermore, precision at top-K for an unsupervised model requires ranking test instances by reconstruction error — which requires that the test set is not randomly split from temporally correlated data (see issue 3).
all_issues_raised: Unsupervised autoencoder requires a reconstruction error threshold that must be either set without labels (defeating labeled evaluation) or tuned on labeled validation data (making the method semi-supervised, not unsupervised)
MTTF as the primary metric is an operational deployment metric that requires temporal ordering of predictions — it is not computable from a randomly shuffled static holdout evaluation
Stratified random split destroys the temporal structure of pre-fault degradation trajectories and corrupts the normal operating distribution used to train the autoencoder
Threshold-based baseline sophistication is unspecified — static setpoint vs. adaptive multivariate statistical process control have very different performance characteristics against autoencoders
Precision at top-K secondary metric is underspecified — K is not defined and temporal ordering destroyed by random split makes ranking by reconstruction error operationally undefined

critic_raw: 1. OVERSAMPLING TRAINING DATA BUT NOT VALIDATION/TEST CREATES ASYMMETRIC EVALUATION: The design oversamples dropout instances in the training data to address class imbalance. This is a legitimate approach for training, but the design does not specify whether the validation and test sets are also oversampled or kept at the natural class distribution. If the validation set is oversampled, model selection is based on performance under an artificial class distribution that does not reflect deployment conditions — the selected threshold and hyperparameters will be miscalibrated for the natural imbalance. If the test set is oversampled, the reported AUPRC will be over-optimistic. The correct procedure is to oversample only the training data and evaluate on the natural class distribution in validation and test. This is not specified. 2. STRATIFIED RANDOM SPLIT ON LONGITUDINAL STUDENT INTERACTION DATA WITH TEMPORAL DROPOUT PATTERNS: Student dropout in online learning has strong temporal structure: dropout rates peak at course start (enrollment bounce), at assignment due dates, and at mid-course difficulty spikes. A random split will place interaction logs from the same student at different time points in different splits. This is doubly problematic: the NLP model trained on early interaction text from a student will be evaluated on the same student's later interactions, creating student-level memorization. More critically, temporal patterns in interaction that predict dropout — decreasing engagement frequency, shift in forum sentiment — are destroyed by random assignment of timestamped events. 3. BERT FOR STUDENT INTERACTION TEXT IN ONLINE LEARNING IS AN ARCHITECTURE-DATA MISMATCH: The design applies a BERT-based NLP model to 'interaction text data' described as 'course access frequency, forum participation, and assignment engagements.' These features are primarily behavioral and tabular — frequency counts, timestamps, categorical engagement types — not natural language text suitable for BERT encoding. Forum participation may contain text, but course access frequency and assignment engagement are numerical or categorical behavioral signals. BERT's pre-training is optimized for natural language understanding from continuous text; applying it to short forum posts alongside numerical behavioral features requires a specific multi-modal architecture that is not described. If BERT is applied only to forum text and the numerical features are appended separately, the design is implicitly a multi-modal model, which is more complex to train and evaluate than stated. 4. LOGISTIC REGRESSION ON DEMOGRAPHICS ONLY IS A TRIVIALLY WEAK BASELINE: The baseline model uses 'only demographic attributes.' Student age, gender, geographic region, and educational background have weak and inconsistent predictive power for online course dropout compared to behavioral engagement signals. This baseline is not representative of state-of-the-art educational data mining approaches — strong baselines for dropout prediction include gradient-boosted models on engineered behavioral features (access frequency, recency, assignment completion rates), which consistently outperform demographic-only logistic regression by a wide margin. Beating a demographic-only baseline does not establish that the NLP model captures useful additional signal — it establishes only that behavioral engagement signals (including the NLP features) contain more information than demographics. 5. AUPRC AS PRIMARY METRIC IS APPROPRIATE BUT THRESHOLD-FREE — OPERATIONAL EARLY IDENTIFICATION REQUIRES A FIXED INTERVENTION POINT: The design selects AUPRC as the primary metric, which is appropriate for imbalanced binary classification. However, the stated success criterion is 'early identification of at-risk students' — an operational goal that requires a fixed decision threshold at a specific point in the course timeline. AUPRC integrates performance across all thresholds, including cases where predictions are made with complete course interaction data (near the course end), which provides no early warning value. The design does not specify at what point during the course the prediction is made or whether the model is evaluated only on early-course interaction data, where the operational value of early identification is realized.
all_issues_raised: Oversampling is applied to training but the design does not specify natural class distribution is preserved in validation and test, creating asymmetric evaluation under artificial imbalance
Stratified random split destroys temporal dropout patterns and creates student-level leakage for students with interaction logs spanning the data collection period

AUPRC integrates over all thresholds including post-course predictions — early identification value requires evaluation at a specific early-course intervention point that is not specified
